blocks.statement: I can state that total energy (KE + PE + thermal + ...) is ALWAYS conserved, and identify thermal energy as the destination of "lost" ME from friction or inelastic collisions.

blocks.whatWeKnow: 2026-XJ's impact KE becomes heat — enough to vaporize rock, raise tsunamis, ignite forests.
blocks.connection: Unit 5 picks up here. Today's bridge: heat is the form that ME usually becomes when it's "lost."

blocks.markdown:
## The grand rule

ME conservation (KE + PE = constant) only holds when no friction acts. But friction doesn't DESTROY energy — it relocates it. The full ledger:

**KE + PE + thermal + sound + light + chemical + ... = constant. ALWAYS.**

ME conservation is a SPECIAL CASE of this broader rule — the case where the thermal column stays empty.

When friction is in play, the bookkeeping gains one term:

**PE_top + KE_top = KE_bottom + PE_bottom + Q_thermal**

Whatever ME goes missing shows up as Q — the heat generated. Yesterday's 'lost' collision KE and today's friction losses all land in the same place: random molecular motion.

terms.definition: The energy of random molecular motion. When friction or inelastic collisions "remove" ME, that energy goes into raising the temperature of the objects involved. Rubbing your hands together: 50 N of friction over 0.5 m → 25 J of thermal energy → hands warm.
terms.cognate: Sp. energía térmica · Pt. energia térmica · HC enèji tèmik

terms.definition: The grand principle: the SUM of all energy forms (KE + PE + thermal + radiant + sound + chemical + ...) is conserved in any closed system. ALWAYS. ME conservation is a SPECIAL CASE — when no friction; total energy conservation is the broader rule. A sled slides down a hill and stops: PE_top = thermal generated by friction (plus a tiny bit of sound).
terms.cognate: Sp. conservación de energía total · Pt. conservação da energia total

blocks.title: Heat is NOT temperature
blocks.markdown: Heat is the ENERGY that flows; temperature is the AVERAGE motion of molecules. Same heat input, different temperature rise — depending on the material and how much of it there is.

blocks.title: The sled's energy, start to stop
blocks.caption: No energy disappears. The PE that started at the top ends up, Joule for Joule, as thermal energy in the snow and runners.

links.label: Thermal energy in snow + runners
links.sublabel: friction converts ALL of it — sled stops

blocks.prompt: SLED SLIDES AND STOPS. A 50-kg sled at the top of a hill (h = 8 m, friction-free start) slides 12 m down a snowy slope and stops at the bottom (friction at the end zone). How much heat was generated?
blocks.given: m = 50 kg · h = 8 m · g = 9.81 · v_top = v_bot = 0
blocks.equation: Total energy: PE_top + KE_top = KE_bot + PE_bot + Q_thermal
blocks.answer: ≈ 3924 J of heat generated. All the starting PE became thermal energy.

blocks.title: Preview of Unit 5
blocks.markdown: Unit 5 will tell us how MUCH temperature rise a given heat input produces — and that depends on the material. Steel needs much less heat per °C than water.

blocks.prompt: FRICTION ON A HAND-RUB. You rub your hands together. The friction force averages 50 N, you move them 0.5 m of relative distance, back and forth 10 times. How much heat do you generate? (Hint: total distance = 10 × 0.5 m × 2 each pass.)
blocks.givenHint: F = 50 N · 0.5 m per stroke · 10 back-and-forth passes, each pass covering 2 × 0.5 m.

blocks.prompt: A 50-kg sled slides 12 m down a hill (8 m drop) and stops. How much heat was generated by friction? Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: When a sled slides to a stop, the "missing" energy became ___. Total energy is ___ — just in a different ___.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **9.9–9.11** (efficiency and energy dissipated as heat, energy for life, sources of energy). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
